title: Herb and Parmesan scones
subtitle: Savoury scones with fresh chives, thyme, and finely grated Parmesan
excerpt: Eight savoury scones made with self-raising flour, cold butter, Parmesan, and a handful of fresh chives and thyme. Season generously — savoury scone dough needs more salt than you think. Good alongside soup or a cheese board.
sourceNotes: Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the savoury scone chapter documents a plain scone dough enriched with grated cheese and fresh or dried herbs as a savoury accompaniment. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the cheese biscuit section documents a similar dough for savoury enriched scones. The herb and Parmesan combination follows the public-domain British savoury scone tradition.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze baked cooled scones in a bag. Reheat in a 180°C oven for 8 minutes.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best on the day. The dough can be made, cut, and refrigerated unbaked for up to 4 hours — bake straight from the fridge.

glossaryTerms.definition: Tasting and correcting the seasoning of a savoury scone dough before baking. Savoury doughs require noticeably more salt than sweet ones because the scone is eaten without sweet accompaniments. An underseasoned savoury scone tastes bland regardless of the cheese or herb quality. Taste a small pinch of raw dough — it should taste lightly salty, not neutral. If not, add a further pinch of salt and mix briefly.

content.text: Savoury scones live or die on seasoning. The Parmesan adds saltiness and a sharp, nutty flavour, but scone dough needs more salt than you might expect — it absorbs salt less efficiently than bread. Taste the dough before cutting. Practice
here: a pinch of raw dough should taste gently salty, not neutral.

items.prepNote: finely grated — keep 25 g aside for the tops

content.text: Heat the oven to 220°C fan. Combine the {{self-raising-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, {{sea-salt-fine}}, and {{black-pepper-ground}} in a bowl. Add the {{unsalted-butter}} cold butter and rub in until the mixture resembles coarse breadcrumbs. Stir in 50 g of the {{parmesan}} Parmesan, the {{chives}}, and {{thyme-fresh}} thyme. Add the {{whole-milk}} cold milk and mix briefly with a round-bladed knife until the dough just comes together. Taste a small pinch of raw dough — if it tastes bland, add a further pinch of salt.

Turn the dough onto a lightly floured surface and pat to 3 cm thick. Cut with a 6 cm cutter pressing straight down without twisting. Place on a lined baking tray. Brush tops with {{whole-milk}} a little milk and scatter the reserved {{parmesan}} Parmesan over each. Bake for 12–13 minutes until the scones have risen and the Parmesan on top is golden and beginning to crisp at the edges. Cool for 5 minutes on a rack before eating — these are excellent warm alongside a bowl of soup.
